verses.text: Nadab and Abihu took their censers, put fire and incense — strange fire which the LORD commanded not. Fire from the LORD devoured them; they died before Him. Moses to Aaron: I will be sanctified in them that come nigh Me, and before the people I will be glorified. Aaron held his peace. Guidance: The same fire that accepted the offering in chapter 9 destroys those who improvise in chapter 10. Holiness is not at our disposal. Aaron's silence is the only honest response.

Moses called Mishael and Elzaphan: carry your brethren outside the camp. They carried them in their coats. Moses to Aaron, Eleazar, Ithamar: rend not your clothes — lest ye die. Israel may bewail. Go not from the door — the anointing oil is on you. Guidance: Aaron and his two surviving sons cannot mourn. The anointing oil binds them at the altar even on the day their family burns. Israel grieves for them; the priests stand still.

The LORD to Aaron: drink no wine nor strong drink when ye go into the tabernacle — that ye die not. A statute forever. That ye may distinguish holy and unholy, clean and unclean; teach Israel all the statutes the LORD spoke. Guidance: A sober ministry. Some readers think Nadab and Abihu were drunk. God draws the line: no alcohol when you enter His house. The priest's job is to distinguish — and you cannot judge what is holy when your judgment is dulled.

Moses to Aaron and his sons: take the meat offering that remains of the LORD's fire offerings; eat unleavened beside the altar — most holy. The wave breast and heave shoulder eat in a clean place — your sons' and daughters' due from Israel's peace offerings. Guidance: After the funeral, the meal must still be eaten. Grief does not cancel the priest's portion; ministry has to continue. Sometimes faithfulness is just doing what you were given to do next.

Moses searched for the goat of sin offering; behold, burnt. Angry with Eleazar and Ithamar: why ate ye not in the holy place? It is most holy. Aaron: today they offered sin and burnt offerings — and such have befallen me. If I had eaten today, would it have been accepted? Moses heard, was content. Guidance: Aaron speaks for the first time since his sons died. Grief made the meal impossible. Moses' anger melts before Aaron's honesty. Even law bends before a father's weeping heart.
